mechanism_narrative: Consistent increases in nonfarm payrolls (PAYEMS) signal sustained labor market expansion, which raises aggregate demand and corporate earnings, producing positive S&P 500 forward returns over the next 60 trading days. The signal is always active: any monthly increase in PAYEMS predicts positive forward returns.
evidence_refs: fact:Historical correlation between payroll growth and equity returns
failure_condition: Rolling 60-day forward returns in months with PAYEMS > previous month are not significantly above zero (one-sided t-test p > 0.10) over a 5-year backtest.

mechanism_narrative: When the federal funds rate falls to or below 0.25%, the market interprets this as a strong accommodative posture from the Federal Reserve, compressing discount rates and boosting equity valuations. This threshold trigger produces positive S&P 500 returns over the following 20 trading days.
failure_condition: Average 20-day forward return after FEDFUNDS first reaches <= 0.25% is negative or statistically indistinguishable from zero (one-sample t-test p > 0.05).

mechanism_narrative: The joint behavior of retail sales (RSAFS) and housing starts (HOUST) provides a composite measure of consumer and residential demand strength. When both rise month-over-month simultaneously, it signals robust final demand that lifts corporate earnings and drives short-term positive S&P 500 returns over 5 trading days.
failure_condition: In months where both RSAFS and HOUST increase, the 5-day forward return for S&P 500 is negative or has a win rate below 50% over any 3-year rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: Industrial production (INDPRO) rising from its trough after a recession signals that manufacturing and supply capacity are recovering, leading to stronger economic growth and positive S&P 500 returns over the next 60 trading days. This mechanism is active only during the post-shock recovery regime, defined as the first 12 months after the NBER cycle trough.
regime_hint: post-shock recovery regime (first 12 months after cycle trough)
failure_condition: During the post-recession recovery regime, INDPRO increases do not predict positive 60-day forward returns with a mean > 0 and win rate > 55% over all historical recessions since 1970.

mechanism_narrative: A declining unemployment rate (UNRATE) reflects tightening labor markets that support wage growth and consumer spending, which in turn lifts S&P 500 earnings. This trend-following signal is always active: any decrease in UNRATE from the prior month predicts positive forward returns over the next 20 trading days.
failure_condition: Months with a decrease in UNRATE do not produce mean 20-day forward return > 0 and win rate > 52% over a 10-year walk-forward period.

mechanism_narrative: When housing starts (HOUST) exceed 1.5 million units annualized, it signals a strong housing market that drives construction employment, consumer spending, and banking sector strength. This threshold trigger predicts positive S&P 500 returns over the next 20 trading days.
failure_condition: After HOUST exceeds 1.5M, the average 20-day forward SPX return is not significantly positive (p > 0.05) or the win rate is below 55% in out-of-sample testing.

mechanism_narrative: Joint increases in nonfarm payrolls (PAYEMS) and retail sales (RSAFS) provide a powerful composite signal of labor income and consumption. When both rise month-over-month, this interaction drives a strong demand impulse that lifts S&P 500 returns over the next 5 trading days, as markets react to the combined strength in the two highest-frequency demand indicators.
failure_condition: When both PAYEMS and RSAFS increase sequentially, the 5-day forward return for S&P 500 has a mean <= 0 or a win rate <= 50% over any complete business cycle.

mechanism_narrative: During a monetary easing cycle, the combination of low and declining federal funds rate (FEDFUNDS) alongside recovering real GDP growth (GDPC1) signals that accommodative policy is successfully stimulating the economy. This regime-conditional mechanism predicts positive S&P 500 returns over the next 60 trading days. The easing cycle is defined as a period where FEDFUNDS is lower than 6 months prior and GDPC1 is accelerating (sequential quarterly increase).
candidate_trigger: FEDFUNDS < value 6 months prior AND GDPC1 quarterly change > previous quarter change
regime_hint: monetary easing cycle with emerging growth acceleration
failure_condition: In easing-plus-acceleration regimes, the 60-day forward SPX return is not positive on average, or the null hypothesis of zero mean return cannot be rejected at the 5% significance level over all historical instances since 1960.
